title: Edge AI Basics
lesson:
Edge AI means running an AI model on the device where the data is created or used — a phone, a camera, a car, a factory sensor, a smart speaker — instead of sending the data over the internet to a big computer in a data-centre. The "edge" is simply the outer edge of the network: the spot closest to the real world, far away from the central servers in the middle.

Edge AI is the practical side of two ideas you may have already met: On-Device AI (the model lives on a personal gadget) and Local Inference (running the model yourself rather than calling someone else's API).

Cloud AI vs edge AI

Most AI you have used works like ordering takeaway: you type a message, it travels to a giant kitchen (the cloud), gets cooked on powerful machines, and the result is delivered back. That kitchen has near-unlimited power, but you pay per order, you wait for delivery, and the restaurant sees everything you ordered.

Edge AI is cooking at home: a smaller kitchen, but the food is instant, free per meal, works when the internet is down, and nobody else sees your recipe. Here is the same trade-off laid out plainly:

Cloud AI (data-centre)Edge AI (on the device)
Huge models, near-unlimited computeSmall models that fit the device
Round-trip latency (network delay there and back)Instant — no network hop
Needs internetWorks fully offline
Data leaves the deviceData stays put — better privacy
Pay per request, foreverNo per-request bill once shipped
Uses someone else's powerUses the device's own battery and heat budget

Latency is the time you wait for an answer. On the edge, a face-unlock or a "hey, wake up" command must reply in a few milliseconds — far too fast to wait for a server. That speed, plus privacy and offline use, is why edge AI exists.

The hard constraint: a tiny kitchen

Edge devices are weak compared to a data-centre. A phone might have a few gigabytes of memory and must not get hot or flatten the battery in ten minutes. So the central challenge of edge AI is squeezing a useful model into a small, power-stingy box. Three tools do almost all the heavy lifting:

  • Small models. Pick a Small Language Model (an SLM — a model with far fewer parameters, the learned numbers inside it) instead of a giant one. Fewer numbers means less memory and less work.
  • Quantization. Shrink each number to fewer bits (see Quantization). A 4-bit model is roughly a quarter the size of the 16-bit original — often the difference between fitting on a phone and not.
  • Hardware accelerators. Modern phones and edge boards ship a special chip called an NPU (Neural Processing Unit) — a calculator built only for the kind of maths AI does. It is much faster and more power-efficient at that one job than the general-purpose CPU/GPU.

Analogy two: the espresso machine, not the plantation

A data-centre model is a sprawling coffee plantation that can supply a whole country. An edge model is the espresso machine on your kitchen counter. You will never grow beans with it, but it makes your coffee instantly, privately, and without phoning the plantation. The skill of edge AI is choosing a machine small enough for your counter that still makes coffee you actually want to drink.

How it runs in practice

You rarely run raw weights on an edge device. You first convert the model into a compact, hardware-friendly format and runtime. Common ones in 2026:

  • ONNX Runtime and LiteRT (formerly TensorFlow Lite) — Android and embedded boards.
  • Core ML — Apple devices.
  • GGUF files run by llama.cpp — portable local LLMs.

A typical Apple-side conversion looks like this:

import coremltools as ct

# Convert a trained model into Apple's on-device format
mlmodel = ct.convert(
    traced_model,                       # your model, ready to export
    compute_units=ct.ComputeUnit.ALL,   # let it use CPU + GPU + NPU
    minimum_deployment_target=ct.target.iOS18,
)
mlmodel.save("Classifier.mlpackage")    # ship this inside the app

The key line is compute_units=ALL: it tells the device to use the NPU when it can, falling back to CPU otherwise. That one flag is often the difference between a snappy feature and a battery-draining one.

Real edge wins today include phone keyboards predicting your next word, cameras that blur backgrounds on-device, cars reading road signs without signal, and hearing aids cleaning up speech in real time — all places where a network round-trip would be too slow, too private, or simply unavailable.
mentalModel: Edge AI is cooking at home instead of ordering takeaway: a smaller kitchen on the device itself, but the food is instant, free per meal, works with no internet, and nobody else sees your recipe.
takeaways: Edge AI runs the model on the device, not in the cloud.
You win on latency, privacy, offline use, and per-request cost.
The catch: tiny memory and battery — use small, quantized models on the NPU.

diagram.svg: <svg class="dg" viewBox="0 0 720 340" role="img" aria-label="Cloud AI versus edge AI for one request"><defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="12" markerHeight="12" refX="9" refY="5" orient="auto"><path class="dg-arrowhead" d="M0,0 L11,5 L0,10 z"/></marker></defs><text class="dg-title" x="360" y="34" text-anchor="middle">Same request, two paths</text><rect class="dg-box" x="40" y="130" width="130" height="70" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="105" y="160" text-anchor="middle">Your device</text><text class="dg-sub" x="105" y="182" text-anchor="middle">phone / camera</text><line class="dg-edge" x1="170" y1="110" x2="250" y2="80" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><text class="dg-note" x="210" y="82" text-anchor="middle">over internet</text><rect class="dg-box dg-box-warn" x="250" y="50" width="170" height="66" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="335" y="78" text-anchor="middle">Cloud data-centre</text><text class="dg-sub" x="335" y="100" text-anchor="middle">huge model, pay per call</text><line class="dg-edge" x1="420" y1="83" x2="560" y2="83" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect class="dg-box-warn dg-box" x="560" y="50" width="130" height="66" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="625" y="78" text-anchor="middle">Answer back</text><text class="dg-sub" x="625" y="100" text-anchor="middle">100s of ms</text><line class="dg-edge" x1="170" y1="200" x2="250" y2="245" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect class="dg-box dg-box-accent" x="250" y="230" width="170" height="66" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="335" y="258" text-anchor="middle">On-device NPU</text><text class="dg-sub" x="335" y="280" text-anchor="middle">small, quantized model</text><line class="dg-edge" x1="420" y1="263" x2="560" y2="263" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect class="dg-box dg-box-good" x="560" y="230" width="130" height="66" rx="12"/><text class="dg-label" x="625" y="258" text-anchor="middle">Answer back</text><text class="dg-sub" x="625" y="280" text-anchor="middle">few ms, offline</text></svg>
diagram.caption: The same request can travel to the cloud and back, or stay on the device — edge AI takes the lower, faster, private path.

mistakes: Trying to fit a giant model on a tiny device. A 70-billion-parameter model will not run on a phone, full stop. Start from a small model and quantize it — don't try to cram the data-centre into your pocket.

Forgetting the battery and heat budget. A model can be fast for one query and still cook the phone or flatten the battery if run continuously. On the edge, energy per inference matters as much as raw speed.

Skipping the conversion step. Raw PyTorch weights won't use the NPU. You must convert to Core ML, LiteRT, ONNX, or GGUF for the device's accelerator to kick in — otherwise everything crawls on the CPU.

Assuming the NPU is always used. If even one operation in the model isn't supported by the accelerator, the runtime may quietly fall back to the slow CPU. Profile on a real device and check which chip is actually doing the work.

Ignoring update logistics. A cloud model is fixed in one place; an edge model is copied onto millions of devices. Shipping a new version means an app update for everyone — plan for versioning and slow rollouts.

Testing only on a high-end device. Your flagship phone is not your users' three-year-old budget phone. A feature that flies on the newest hardware can be unusably slow on the cheap devices most people actually own.
exercise:
Download a small quantized chat model in GGUF format (for example a 1B–3B parameter model in 4-bit) and run it locally with llama.cpp or Ollama on your own laptop — no internet connection allowed during the run. Ask it three questions and time how long each answer takes from the moment you hit enter.

Stretch goal: Pull your network cable / turn off Wi-Fi and confirm it still works, then watch your laptop's CPU and memory usage (Activity Monitor or Task Manager) while it generates. Note the peak memory the model uses, and estimate whether that footprint would fit inside a phone with, say, 6 GB of RAM shared with everything else.
solution: Hint: A 1B–3B model in 4-bit typically needs roughly 1–2 GB of memory and, on a modern laptop CPU, answers short questions in a second or two — the first token may lag slightly while the model loads. It will absolutely keep working offline, because the whole model is sitting on your disk; that is the entire point of edge AI. On the memory question: if the model alone eats ~1.5 GB, that's plausible on a 6 GB phone, but remember the operating system and other apps also need RAM, so on the edge you usually lean toward the smallest model that still does the job, and let the NPU handle the maths to save battery.
summary: Edge AI runs models on the device itself — phone, camera, car, sensor — instead of in the cloud, winning on latency, privacy, offline use, and per-request cost. The catch is a tiny power-and-memory budget, so you reach for small models, quantization, and the device's NPU accelerator, then convert to a hardware-friendly format like Core ML, LiteRT, ONNX, or GGUF. The real skill is fitting a useful-enough model into the small kitchen you've got.

keyTerms.def: Running an AI model on the device where data is created or used, rather than sending data to a central cloud server.
The outer edge of the network — the devices closest to the real world (phones, cameras, sensors), far from central data-centres.
The time you wait for an answer. Edge AI cuts the network round-trip, so responses can be near-instant.
A chip built specifically for the maths AI models do — much faster and more power-efficient at that one job than a general CPU or GPU.
Storing a model's numbers with fewer bits (e.g. 4-bit instead of 16-bit) to shrink its memory footprint so it fits on small devices.
Turning trained weights into a compact, device-friendly runtime format (Core ML, LiteRT, ONNX, GGUF) so the hardware accelerator can run it.
Popular on-device runtimes: Core ML for Apple, LiteRT (formerly TensorFlow Lite) for Android/embedded, ONNX Runtime for cross-platform deployment.
The energy and heat a device can spend before draining its battery or overheating — a hard limit edge models must respect.
Producing AI results with no internet connection, because the entire model lives on the device itself.

quiz.q: What single fact best defines edge AI?
options: The model runs on the device where the data is, not in a cloud data-centre
The model is always larger than a cloud model
The model can only run when connected to fast internet
The model is trained on the device instead of being downloaded
quiz.explain: Edge AI is about WHERE inference happens — on the local device near the data. Edge models are smaller, run offline, and are usually trained elsewhere then shipped.

quiz.q: A team wants face-unlock that responds in a few milliseconds and never sends face data off the phone. Which approach fits?
options: Call a huge cloud model on every unlock attempt
Run a small on-device model on the phone's NPU
Stream the camera feed to a server for processing
Use the biggest model available regardless of size
quiz.explain: Millisecond latency and keeping face data private both rule out a network round-trip. A small model on the NPU stays fast, offline, and private.

quiz.q: Your converted model runs, but it is slow and drains the battery fast. What is the most likely cause?
options: The model is too small for the device
The internet connection is too slow
Unsupported operations made the runtime fall back to the CPU instead of the NPU
quiz.explain: If even one op isn't supported by the accelerator, the runtime can quietly use the slow, power-hungry CPU. Profile on-device to confirm the NPU is actually doing the work.

quiz.q: Why convert a model to GGUF, Core ML, LiteRT, or ONNX before deploying to the edge?
options: To make the model larger so it is more accurate
To add an internet connection requirement
To package it in a compact, hardware-friendly format the device accelerator can run
To automatically retrain it on the device's data
quiz.explain: Raw training weights won't use the NPU. Conversion produces a compact runtime format the device's accelerator understands — without it, everything crawls on the CPU.
